evidence.page_title: Campaigns that changed the world - LinkedIn
evidence.title: Campaigns that changed the world
evidence.description: A fantastic new initiative by the Chartered Institute of Public Relations. A competion to identify the best global PR campaigns since the foundation of the Institute.
evidence.text:
Campaigns that changed the world
A fantastic new initiative by the Chartered Institute of Public Relations. A competion to identify the best global PR campaigns since the foundation of the Institute. To celebrate their 75th anniversary they want people to consider and vote on #CampaignsThatChangedTheWorld from the eight decades after 1948 to find the one that “best represents the enduring power of public relations in driving positive change and shaping history”. There are powerful examples - from the most recent - the UK vaccination campaign, which led the world, to the launch of the iPhone, the first Earth Day and the Paris Climate agreement and how the campaign for AIDS research and treatment changed the way drugs were identified and tested and shifted perceptions of LGBTQ+ issues
I was privileged to be asked to nominate one for the 1960s. I nominated the Civil Rights Movement in the United States. At the heart of this was Martin Luther King’s 1963 “I have a dream” speech at the Lincoln Memorial, the culmination of the 250,000 person March on Washington for civil rights. The Washington March came after the Freedom Rides and Birmingham campaign and instrumental in persuading President Kennedy that legislative action was needed
The 1960s were a time of change. The Cold War was at its height and technology was changing society. Across the world social reformers made progress. In the US, the civil rights movement generated a new approach to campaigning which led to three significant pieces of legislation Civil Rights (1964), Voting Rights (1965) and Fair Housing (1968). A significant influence on political change was the series of campaigns which employed high profile, creative, mass protests which targeted injustice. The bravery of the campaigners and leadership of Martin Luther King Jnr left both a legacy and inspiration to future generations.
Recommended by LinkedIn
There’s the opportunity to vote and great examples from eight decades, so vote for the one you believe to be the most influential in shaping society and changing the world.
image_captions: How to Experience this American Transfer of Presidential Power
Are we forgetting our role in society?
MLK and Civil Rights Act of 1964

evidence.page_title: Online activism could do with a little more empathy - LinkedIn
evidence.title: Online activism could do with a little more empathy
evidence.description: As noted by Trevor Noah, everything that has been going on in the world, has been going on against the backdrop of a global pandemic, which has confined people to their homes with no end date; has caused many to lose their loved ones, others their jobs; and which has left many facing the mental stru
evidence.text:
Online activism could do with a little more empathy
As noted by Trevor Noah, everything that has been going on in the world, has been going on against the backdrop of a global pandemic, which has confined people to their homes with no end date; has caused many to lose their loved ones, others their jobs; and which has left many facing the mental struggles that come with the socio-economic uncertainty of these times.
While social media outlets cannot be considered a barometer of our society – given the self-selection of their consumer base and the subsequent one of their ‘active’ users – these platforms do, to a considerable extent, reflect wider socio-political trends, in ever-growing echo chambers aided by the power of algorithms.
Without a doubt, the public outcry that followed George Floyd’s death was not only imperative, but also a force of change. It shouted: in the 21st century racism is not okay and will not be tolerated.
In some way, if there was a time to bring attention to racism most effectively, this was the time. Not that any life is worth sacrificing or is quantifiable in any way. However, this time, people have been watching, listening and speaking up.
Yet, while recent events attest to humanity’s gradual, painful and non-continuous progress towards enlightenment, scratching through the surface has, at times, revealed a duller reality: many people care, but not enough to fully cast their ego aside.
On 2 June MTV kick-started ‘Blackout Tuesday’ by silencing its channel for 8 minutes and 46 seconds – the length of time a white policeman knelt on a black man’s neck, causing his death. All for allegedly having a counterfeit＄20 note. Other channels followed suit, including the music industry which halted business for a day.
#TheShowMustBePaused: let’s disconnect from work and reconnect with our communities. Let’s take this time to listen, understand and educate ourselves.
Soon, the wider Instagram community took to the platform to share black squares, so as to manifest solidarity and - one would hope - take this time to learn about racial discrimination and ways to combat it. Even those who generally shy away from politically loaded and ‘heavy’ conversations, felt comfortable enough to partake.
Later however, many took to the platform to educate users that sharing black squares with the ‘blacklivesmatter’ hashtag was clogging it, blocking “vital” channels of information. Which is an interesting observation, given that Instagram users generally do not seek their information through hashtags, but rather, through the posts and stories that land on their feed; shared by the celebrities whom they actively chose to follow, and whom they consider worthwhile their time.
Let’s get real – what is really at stake here is not equality, but people’s ego. This is not a race against racism, it’s a race to ensure we’re not left behind the trend of ‘political correctness’ and ‘wokeness’. Because woke is a thing now, and most importantly, it’s a testament to one’s IQ. And no one wants to be considered thick - whatever that may mean.
But people’s critique of others’ activism did not end here. In fact, shortly after, many took to social media to condemn the violence and looting present in some Black Lives Matter protests, noting: ‘while I condemn racism, I also condemn violence’ or ‘neither racism nor looting are okay’.
Now, just like protestors should pick their actions wisely, so should people pick their words. Because saying ‘neither racism nor violence are okay’ inherently juxtaposes white racists to black vandals – an inference (and stance) that overlooks some key facts and considerations:
1. Some vandals and looters are white.
2. Vandals exploit any opportunity to wreak havoc, anywhere. This is not a Black Lives Matter characteristic, it’s a human characteristic.
3. Black Lives Matter protestors have heavily condemned violent people for exploiting and disrupting their cause. Critics are not adding anything to the discourse.
4. US law already protects shopkeepers and institutions from vandals and looters. It does not, however, protect black people from senseless acts of killing. The latter needs fighting for, the former doesn’t. The battle you pick matters.
5. Is the goal of a protest not that of causing disruption? Do people not resort to protest when all other means fail? Before condemning violent protest, let’s question its very context, nature and meaning. Alternatively, all historical acts of violence in the name of equality, justice and freedom, should be called into question.
In a world where knowledge is frail, it’s surprising how many people are quick to make judgements. Especially when it comes to complex social phenomena that comprise a number of visible and invisible factors.
Maybe, if we take a step back, we can start fighting injustice with kindness towards one another; setting aside this constant need to elevate our moral status above theirs; accepting equality, for once.
Those speaking up against racism need support, not competition. Be they the ones protesting on the streets; sharing black squares on Instagram with the wrong hashtag; speaking up online but struggling in person; or the ones silent online but active at home, having difficult conversations with family. Some may not even be doing anything, but may be the ones who consciously and subconsciously treat all humans equally, on a day-to-day basis, irrespective of their race, creed or culture. The ones whose actions speak louder than their words.
Knowledge is frail and perspective matters. Let’s be humble; think twice; empathise with others; listen more than we speak; accept equal moral status; and most of all, let our curiosity drive us more than our ego. Shared human existence is much more interesting that our individual one.
“Every external interest inspires some activity which, so long as the interest remains alive, is a complete preventive of ennui. Interest in oneself, on the contrary, leads to no activity of a progressive kind. […] External discipline is the only road to happiness for those unfortunates whose self-absorption is too profound to be cured in any other way.” Bertrand Russell
*Posting purely in a personal capacity.

evidence.title: #ReadOurSigns
evidence.description: By Magaly Marques The best part of a protest or demonstration is to witness the creativity with which people express their motives for being there: the signs! At yesterday’s Women’s Anniversary March in Los Angeles, we could see the difference between what motivated people in the 2017 Women’s March
evidence.text:
#ReadOurSigns
By Magaly Marques
The best part of a protest or demonstration is to witness the creativity with which people express their motives for being there: the signs! At yesterday’s Women’s Anniversary March in Los Angeles, we could see the difference between what motivated people in the 2017 Women’s March and what led people to the streets this weekend.
“Still here, Not going anywhere”, was the most obvious sign of resistance to the unchanged status quo. But, because things do change in spite of the backlashes that keep coming against our daily feminism, we also saw signs that read: “Build this Wall – between Church and State”, “We are All Women, Trans, Immigrants, LGBTQIA, Black Women, People of Color, etc.”, “Health Care is a Human Right”, “Boys will be Good Humans”, and “Patriarch Won’t just Implode”.
In one year, protesters in the U.S. went from yelling “Not my President” to declaring that the oppressive environment we live in today –and one that has been building up for decades of liberal, conservative, racist investment in reinforcing barriers to feminism and human rights—, must change. This time, we are not back on the streets to demand our right to vote. That’s what our great-grandmothers accomplished at the turn of the 19th century. Now, we are making it explicit that by voting we expect our vote to count, our voice to be heard, and our platforms to be taken seriously. Demonstrators don’t get up at the crack of dawn on a weekend to endure subway lines, walk several miles and stand up in unfriendly weather for peanuts. Do we do it for the collective energy that we unquestionably absorb by being amongst folks who, like us, believe that the pursuit of justice and happiness is worthy of our efforts? Yes. Most definitely. However, it might be more accurate to say that we do it to demonstrate how serious we are when we say “Enough is Enough”, and how powerful our movement for “Rights, Justice, and the Rule of Law” is.
Suffragettes didn’t stop until women voted, until it became normal and no big deal that women voted. And in the process we normalized birth control, women in higher education, and women in politics. Still, it does happen that those who regret the fact that, today, women vote are the same people who try to roll back history into its own spool. If in power positions, these people or institutions will attempt to re-write the narrative, re-count the facts, and re-build barriers, walls and gaps between people who are “different” and certain rights and social and scientific benefits that humanity has accumulated over time. So we march. We march because we know that we must claim our gains, our rights and our narrative. And because we must resist the counter efforts that seek to treat rights as privileges.
Of course some of us are tired of protesting the “same old same old &*#!”, and some of us may fear that nothing is really happening. Why would yet another march make a difference? But that’s exactly what we feared before the #MeToo movement when we asked ourselves: Why expose ourselves and break the silence? Why endure yet another humiliation if the power is on the other side? And that’s when we must resort to memory and solidarity to remind each other of how change happens. The moment when a millionaire news anchor, or film producer, or politician, media guru, journalist, or a CEO is finally removed from power because of one too many voices. That’s the shift that matters.
It seems to me that industries and markets, institutions and governments, more than anything want the steady flow of business and resources, even more than they might have wanted to protect their own in the past. Consumers, voters, Millennials, educated young professionals, union workers, students are ready to wear their pink pussy hats at any time. One look out the window or at a TV, phone or computer screen this weekend suffices to a savvy businessperson to conclude that if crowds of people are taking to the streets again to show where they stand, then they should take note of that fact. The time when power was the result of privilege and biology is coming to an end, and will continue to crumble because of people wearing pink pussy hats and demanding justice.
